# April 13, 2026

Global financial markets demonstrated notable resilience on Monday, staging a late-session rally that saw the S&P 500 recover from early geopolitical-driven losses to close up [0.47% at 6,846.43](https://www.reuters.com/world/china/global-markets-wrapup-1-2026-04-12/). This recovery was primarily catalyzed by President Trump’s optimistic public comments suggesting that Iran "wants to make a deal," which significantly eased investor fears of immediate escalation despite the commencement of a [U.S. naval blockade of the Strait of Hormuz](https://www.reuters.com/world/iran-war-live-trump-says-us-begin-naval-blockade-irans-ports-strait-hormuz-2026-04-13/). While energy markets remain under intense pressure—with Brent crude and WTI both surging [above \$100 per barrel](https://www.globalbankingandfinance.com/oil-prices-jump-6-100-barrel-us-blockade-iran/) due to potential supply disruptions—institutional players such as \[BlackRock and Citadel\](https://www.reuters.com/markets/us/wall-streets-earnings- fantasies-may-soon-get-harsh-reality-check-2026-04-13/) have begun re-entering equities, viewing the current valuation reset as a strategic entry point ahead of Q1 earnings. However, the hedge fund sector continues to face structural headwinds, highlighted by the recent [shuttering of Alua Capital Management](https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-04-13/viking-lone-pine-alumni-shutter-2-billion-alua-hedge-fund), underscoring the ongoing challenges for leveraged players in a high-volatility, higher-for-longer rate environment.
